--- title: "Blog Post Template" description: "A starter template showing the Quarto features most useful for data analytics and ML blog posts." author: "Jonathan Pearce" date: "2026-01-01" image: thumbnail.png draft: true toc: true code-fold: true code-copy: true number-sections: true fig-cap-location: bottom execute: echo: true warning: false --- ## Introduction Start with a brief overview of the problem or topic. Use **bold** and *italic* for emphasis, and [links](https://quarto.org) where needed. ::: {.callout-note} ## Key Takeaway Callout boxes are great for highlighting important points. Other types: `.callout-warning`, `.callout-tip`, `.callout-important`. ::: ## Data & Setup ::: {#setup .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code code-summary="Import libraries"} import numpy as np import pandas as pd import matplotlib.pyplot as plt np.random.seed(42) ``` ::: ## Analysis ### Generating Sample Data ::: {#cell-fig-scatter .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} x = np.random.normal(0, 1, 200) y = 0.8 * x + np.random.normal(0, 0.5, 200) plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4)) plt.scatter(x, y, alpha=0.6, edgecolors="k", linewidth=0.5) plt.xlabel("Feature X") plt.ylabel("Target Y") plt.title("Sample Scatter Plot") plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Scatter plot of two correlated variables.](index_files/figure-html/fig-scatter-output-1.png){#fig-scatter} ::: ::: ### Summary Statistics ::: {#tbl-summary .cell tbl-cap='Summary statistics for the sample data.' execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} df = pd.DataFrame({"x": x, "y": y}) df.describe().round(2) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ```{=html}
x y
count 200.00 200.00
mean -0.04 0.01
std 0.93 0.93
min -2.62 -2.59
25% -0.71 -0.61
50% -0.00 0.08
75% 0.50 0.58
max 2.72 2.38
``` ::: ::: ### Tabsets for Comparisons ::: {.panel-tabset} #### Model A ::: {#7bf151b9 .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} from numpy.polynomial import polynomial as P coeffs = np.polyfit(x, y, 1) print(f"Linear fit: y = {coeffs[0]:.3f}x + {coeffs[1]:.3f}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Linear fit: y = 0.850x + 0.045 ``` ::: ::: #### Model B ::: {#46c9a566 .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} coeffs2 = np.polyfit(x, y, 2) print(f"Quadratic fit: y = {coeffs2[0]:.3f}x² + {coeffs2[1]:.3f}x + {coeffs2[2]:.3f}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Quadratic fit: y = -0.049x² + 0.852x + 0.087 ``` ::: ::: ::: ## Mathematical Notation Inline math: $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ Display math: $$ \mathcal{L}(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[y_i \log \hat{y}_i + (1 - y_i)\log(1 - \hat{y}_i)\right] $$ ## Diagrams with Mermaid ```{mermaid} flowchart LR A[Raw Data] --> B[Clean & Transform] B --> C[Feature Engineering] C --> D[Model Training] D --> E[Evaluation] E --> F{Good enough?} F -- Yes --> G[Deploy] F -- No --> C ``` ## Conclusion Summarise findings and next steps here. ## References ::: {#refs} :::
